titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Havelock Ellis papers

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 6, 2024 at 08:33

did.abstract:
The Havelock Ellis papers contain correspondence and manuscripts on literary and psychological subjects, particularly on the topic of human sexuality. Important correspondents are Sigmund Freud, Bertrand Russell, Herbert Spencer, Thomas Hardy, Leo Tolstoy, Amy Lowell, Louisa Sarah Bevington Guggenberger, Rockwell Kent, Upton Sinclair, Maria Gordon, Marcel Jouhandeau, William Morris, Marc-Andre Raffalovich, Francoise Roussel Delisle (Francois Lafitte-Cyon Delisle), and Rebecca West. The bulk of the papers consists of manuscripts by Ellis, of which the longest is "My Confessions". This work is made up of seventy short pieces, each based upon a problem posed to Ellis by a correspondent. The literary essays are on Thomas Hardy's Jude the Obscure, Marcel Jouhandeau and William Morris.
The addition to this collection contains correspondence and writings primarily from Ellis to his biographer, Houston Peterson. The writings are published and unpublished works written by or about Ellis used in Peterson's biography, including a copy of one of Peterson's lectures on Ellis.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: The Havelock Ellis Papers were purchased from the Charles Tuttle Company of Rutland, Vermont, in 1941.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Havelock Ellis Papers, Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Havelock Ellis (1859-1939) was a British essayist, editor, physician, and psychologist. He studied and challenged the prevalent ideas of human sexual behavior beginning with his research for Man and Women (1894) that led to his major work, the seven volume, Studies in the Psychology of Sex (1897-1928). His last writings were essays on literature and art reprinted in Views and Reviews (1932) and his autobiography My Life (1939).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection consists of correspondence and manuscripts of Havelock Ellis. Ellis, 1859-1939 began his career in the medical profession but soon gave up general practice for literary and scientific work. The more popular researches of the Freudian school have to a considerable extent overshadowed the methods of Ellis, in which more emphasis is laid on biology and less on clinical experience.

The more interesting correspondents include William James, Francoise Lafitte-Cyon, Sigmund Freud, Bertrand Russell, poet Louisa Sarah Bevington Guggenberger, Herbert Spencer, Thomas Hardy, Leo Tolstoy, Amy Lowell, Rockwell Kent, Upton Sinclair, Maria Gordon, Marcel Jouhandeau, William Morris, Marc-Andre Raffalovich and Rebecca West. Much of the correspondence consists of editorial matters and complimentary notes. Of greatest interest are the Freud letters. The collection will be of interest to the scholar interested in Ellis and in his relationship to Freud.

The bulk of the collection is composed of manuscripts by Ellis. Among them is a work entitled My Confessional made up of seventy short pieces. Each is based upon a problem posed to Ellis by one of his correspondents so that the author's opinion on a great number of topics is presented. Other manuscripts include: Sex in Contemporary Life, Marcel Jouhandeau, Intro. to James Hinton's Life In Nature (NY 1934), concerning Jude the Obscure, The Problem of Sexual Potency, William Morris.
There is also a manuscript, by Sir T. Percy Nunn entitled Edudation as a Biological Experiment .

otherfindaid.head: Other Finding Aids
otherfindaid.p: For a listing of the outgoing correspondence of Havelock Ellis, please consult theCorrespondence Index.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

did.unittitle: Education as a Biological Experiment (by Sir T. Percy Nunn)
Introduction to James Hinton's Life in Nature

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at beinecke.library@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The bulk of this addition are letters from Havelock Ellis to his biographer, Houston Peterson, who began working on an essay on Ellis in 1923 that turned into his dissertation at Columbia University. This essay was the foundation of Ellis' biography published in 1928. At the time, Ellis was known in the medical field for Sexual Inversion, the first volume of what later became his seven-volume work, Studies in the Psychology of Sex, in which he defined his ideas of sexual and gender non-conformity. Ellis was equally known for his writings in literary criticism and feminism of the early 20th century. The rest of the addition is comprised of Ellis' writings from an early age on the literature he exposed hismelf to and critiqued, as well as his publicatinos in magazines and journals, especialyl relating to women's reproductive rights and early engenics.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The addition is organized into two subseries: Havelock Ellis Correspondence and Writings (1925-1931), and Houston Peterson Papers (1874-1954).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of correspondence from Havelock Ellis to Houston Peterson from 1925-1931 and writings primarily comprised of published and non-published writings from a very young age and throughout his career that include the Bedborough Trial of 1898, which involved an Englishman being arrested for selling Ellis's work Sexual Inversion, in London. Ellis's work had first been published and well received in Germany in 1896. This trial undermined the general publication of the rest of the volumes in English outside of medical publications.

did.unittitle: "What is Pureness", review in Modern Thought
The Mermaid Series responses to the editor from Ellis
Bibliography of the Pschology and Psyschopathology of Vita Sexualis by Dr. Freiherr von Schrenck-Notzing
The Adult Journal Volume 2 Number 6 typed Bedborough Trial
Ellis response to editor of The Lancet

title.part: A Note on the Bedborough Trial

did.unittitle: Eugenics Records Office , Bulletin No. 6, second edition, and notes by Ellis
"An Appreciation of Havelock Ellis", Arthur Symons in The Double-Dealer Magazine , Volume III, No. 14
Prospectus , Saint Thomas Hospital, University of London Medical School
"Heredity of Havelock Ellis", Eugenical News , Volume XI, No. 12
"The Conception of Narcissism", The Psychoanalytic Review , Volume XIV, No. 2
The New Statesman , review of Havelock Ellis by Isaac Goldberg
Ellis Introduction, edited manuscript from Spleen and other stories by Pierre-Victor, Baron de Besenval
"Havelock Ellis Number", Birth Control Review , 2 copies
Miscellaneous notes by Ellis and Peterson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries consists of correspondence from others with a close relationship to Ellis, such as his sister Louie Ellis and his partner Francoise Lafitte-Cyon as well as Vincent Brome, who worked on a subsequent biography of Ellis from 1953-1954, his publishers, and a typed letter assumed to be from Peterson to Ellis. There is a typed manuscript of a lecture series Peterson presented on Ellis.

did.unittitle: Peterson to Ellis and note cards
"Havelock Ellis and Edith Lees", The Times Literary Supplement , by Francois Lafitte-Cyon Delisle (Francoise Roussel Delisle)
